name: Pwyll
description: Pwyll, Prince of Dyfed, is a prominent figure in Welsh mythology, primarily known from the First Branch of the Mabinogi. He is characterized by his initial rashness and subsequent growth into a wise and just ruler. His adventures begin when he exchanges places with Arawn, King of Annwn (the Otherworld), for a year and a day. During this time, Pwyll must uphold Arawn's kingship, defeat his rival Hafgan, and resist the temptation to sleep with Arawn's wife. He succeeds in these tasks, demonstrating his honor and courage. Upon returning to his own kingdom, Pwyll encounters Rhiannon, a beautiful maiden riding a pale horse. He pursues her, eventually winning her hand in marriage. Their son, Gwri Wallt Eurin, is mysteriously stolen shortly after birth, leading to a period of grief and hardship. Pwyll's patience and wisdom are tested throughout these trials, ultimately solidifying his reputation as a respected and just king. He represents concepts of honor, duty, transformation, and the responsibilities of leadership. He is a mortal who navigates the supernatural, learning valuable lessons about kingship and personal growth through his interactions with the Otherworld and its inhabitants.
subtitle: Prince of Dyfed, Lord of Annwn

keyMyths.title: Exchange with Arawn
keyMyths.description: Pwyll exchanges places with Arawn, King of Annwn, for a year and a day. He must maintain Arawn's kingdom and defeat his enemy, Hafgan.

keyMyths.title: Meeting Rhiannon
keyMyths.description: Pwyll encounters Rhiannon, a beautiful maiden on a white horse, and pursues her until she agrees to marry him. Their courtship is marked by supernatural elements.

keyMyths.title: The Stolen Child
keyMyths.description: Pwyll and Rhiannon's son, Gwri Wallt Eurin, is stolen shortly after birth, leading to accusations against Rhiannon and a period of hardship for the couple.

sources.author: Gwyn Jones and Thomas Jones
sources.description: A modern English translation of the Mabinogion.
sources.text: A modern English translation of the Mabinogion.

sources.author: Proinsias Mac Cana
sources.description: An overview of Celtic mythology, including Welsh figures.
